It’s like trying to pick good berries from a bush where some have been spoiled. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Honestly, seeing all this nonsense online, it gives my stomach a little twist, you know? Because I spend so much time looking at these screens—checking on my family, seeing pictures of the garden, even watching some football highlights—it can be very unsettling when you can’t trust what you are seeing. It’s like trying to pick good berries from a bush where some have been spoiled. For me, the key is slowing down, taking a breath, and not letting the anxiety take over when I see something shocking or unbelievable. Before I share anything, even if it makes me very angry or very excited, I try to ask myself a few simple questions, the way I would check the ingredients before I serve a dish at work. Is this coming from someone I truly know? Does this story sound too dramatic to be true, like one of those over-the-top plays in a football match? I find myself looking at the picture itself; sometimes the lighting is strange, or the faces look a little... off. It requires a bit of careful watching, just like when you are birdwatching and need to tell the difference between two similar species. I don't have the fancy education to sift through all the complex computer science behind it, but I have a good instinct, and I trust my instinct. If it feels wrong, if it stirs up that worried feeling in my chest, I just scroll past it. I also try to remember that my friends and family—the real ones, the ones I see over a proper coffee, not just on the screen—they are the ones I trust most. Social media is fun for a little while, like a nice walk in the park, but it shouldn't be where I get all my truth from. I try to keep my interactions kind and calm, even when the content is designed to make people shout at each other.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 75-year-old, highly agreeable, extraverted, yet anxious woman who spends significant time on social media. Her background is lower secondary education, and her daily life involves part-time work and hobbies like gardening and football. The tone must reflect her anxious nature mixed with her high agreeableness and desire for calm. The advice must be practical, relatable to a non-academic background, and framed through her daily experiences (waiting tables, gardening, football). I focused on emphasizing caution, slowing down, relying on instinct, and prioritizing real-life connections over online sensationalism to address the anxiety caused by fake content.
response_parsed.tone: Anxious yet warm, cautious, and highly agreeable
